metadata.description: Kiganda (Luganda) proverbs with English translations, moral themes, and symbolic meanings

entries.english: You reap what you sow.
entries.meaning: Whatever you plant without care, or whatever you invest effort into, that is what you will benefit from. Your outcomes depend on your own actions.

entries.english: One by one makes a bundle.
entries.meaning: Small, consistent efforts accumulate into something great. Never underestimate a small beginning.

entries.english: One who seeks a better life does not put down the hand.
entries.meaning: If you truly want to improve your life, you must never stop working. Persistence is essential.

entries.english: Work like a slave.
entries.meaning: To achieve anything in life, you must work tirelessly without stopping, just as a person under pressure cannot rest until the work is done.

entries.luganda: Akwata empola atuuka wala, nawolovu atuuka emitala
entries.english: One who moves slowly but surely reaches far; the chameleon reaches very far.
entries.meaning: Patience and steady effort lead to greater success than rushing. Taking things carefully is always better than haste.

entries.english: The bone that was not stolen is thrown freely at the front of the house.
entries.meaning: Work for what you have so that you can be free in society. Only what you earned honestly can you enjoy openly.

entries.english: If you wait for the end, you will only pierce the tail.
entries.meaning: Do not procrastinate. Act when the opportunity is right, not after it has passed. Waiting too long means you miss the chance.

entries.english: Teeth together bite the meat.
entries.meaning: When people work together, they achieve more. Unity and teamwork produce the best results.

entries.english: A woman who bears children is better than one who always slaughters a cow.
entries.meaning: Something that gives lasting, living value is better than temporary material wealth. Family and long-term investment matter more than short-term gains.

entries.english: A tiny person carries water further from the well.
entries.meaning: Never underestimate someone because of their size or appearance. What looks small or weak can outperform what looks strong.

entries.english: A foolish person should not make you foolish.
entries.meaning: Never let someone else's poor judgment drag you down. Stay wise even when surrounded by foolishness.

entries.luganda: Nditwaala kinene afa tatuuse ku buko
entries.english: One waiting to get something big dies before the introduction.
entries.meaning: It is better to go with the little you have than to wait for something bigger that may never come. Seize current opportunities.

entries.english: Friendship is like hot fat — enjoy it while it is still warm.
entries.meaning: Relationships and love are sweet at their best moments. Cherish them while they last because they can cool and change.

entries.luganda: Ababiri babibira ebigambo naye abasatu babisatulula
entries.english: Two people keep secrets but three people destroy them.
entries.meaning: The more people know a secret, the harder it is to keep. Be careful who you share private matters with.

entries.luganda: Akatono okalya ne munno bwakwata enkukunyi anyigiramu kinkumu
entries.english: When you share something small with a friend, even a bug gets squeezed in the thumb.
entries.meaning: Share what you have, however small. Sharing is a moral duty even when what you have is very little.

entries.luganda: Gwotazinanga naye bwasituka okuzina nga gwe otuula
entries.english: One you have never danced with — when they start dancing, it is as if you are seated.
entries.meaning: Someone you have not worked or partnered with can overtake you when they start. Do not underestimate newcomers.
symbols: gwotazinanga (one you have never danced with)

entries.english: Rich people love each other (they stay together).
entries.meaning: People of the same level or class tend to associate together. Wealth attracts wealth.

entries.luganda: Amaka abiri musango, bwolwanira agali e kyaggwe nga ge ssingo banyaga
entries.english: Two families are a fight — when you fight for the one in Kyaggwe, the one in Ssingo is taken.
entries.meaning: Managing two households or divided loyalties always leads to trouble. Polygamy and divided attention create endless conflict.
symbols: kyaggwe and ssingo (place names symbolizing two sides)

entries.luganda: Bakuseeredde mu katale oyombera mu kubo
entries.english: You were ripped off in the market but you are arguing about it in the road.
entries.meaning: Solve problems where they happen, not later and elsewhere. Complaining after the moment has passed accomplishes nothing.

entries.english: A small thing that is available is worth more than a big thing that has only been promised.
entries.meaning: Something real and available — even if small — is better than a grand promise that may never be fulfilled.
symbols: ekinene ekisuubize (big thing that is promised)

entries.english: Love feeds on creativity.
entries.meaning: Relationships need constant effort, creativity, and new energy to survive. Love that is not nurtured will die.

entries.english: A visitor is a back-biter.
entries.meaning: Be careful with visitors — when they leave, they will talk about what they saw in your home. Maintain your standards always.

entries.english: One who loses the only thing they have in life stays miserable forever.
entries.meaning: When someone loses their only source of hope or support, their suffering never ends. Guard what you love most.

entries.english: One who has not walked your journey will tell you it is like peeling a banana.
entries.meaning: Someone who has never been through what you have experienced will always make it sound easy. Do not take advice from those with no experience.
symbols: ataalukutambulire (one who won't walk your journey)

entries.luganda: Abasajja kiti kya muwogo wokisuula wekilandira
entries.english: Men are like stems of a cassava plant — wherever you dump them, they grow.
entries.meaning: Men adapt and move on easily. When rejected or left, they simply start again somewhere else.

entries.english: A shoot of a musa banana turns into beer.
entries.meaning: Something that looks small and insignificant today can become something great and valuable in the future. Never write off potential.

entries.luganda: Ssekibojera ku lwazi kyeyesigga mumwa mugumu
entries.english: The bird that pecks at a rock is sure of its strong beak.
entries.meaning: Attempt only what you know you are capable of. Confidence in action comes from knowing your own strength.

entries.english: Over-praising a man can lead him to be eaten by a leopard.
entries.meaning: Too much flattery makes a person overconfident and reckless. Excessive praise can lead someone into dangerous mistakes.

entries.english: One can claim to have many friends until they fall on hard times.
entries.meaning: You do not know who your true friends are until you face difficulty. Hard times reveal the truth about relationships.

entries.english: When money is lost, one stays home.
entries.meaning: A person who loses money becomes lazy and stays home complaining instead of going out to earn more.

entries.english: A small healthy thing is better than a big unhealthy one.
entries.meaning: Quality matters more than size or quantity. A small thing that works well is more valuable than a big thing that does not.

entries.luganda: Okwambala obulungyi ssi kuwasa, ssinga ekajjo lyenjovu lyawangiza muzibu gwazaalampanga
entries.english: Dressing well is not the same as marrying well; do not judge by appearance.
entries.meaning: Appearances can be deceiving. Beautiful clothes or looks do not tell you about a person's character or suitability as a partner.

entries.english: A woman who farms with her husband is not weak.
entries.meaning: A hardworking woman who partners with her husband in all things shows strength, not weakness.

entries.english: One who wrongs you makes you forget God.
entries.meaning: When someone deeply wrongs you, the anger can make you forget your values and want to retaliate. Be careful not to let others drag you away from your principles.

entries.english: One who accepts their mistake pays little.
entries.meaning: Accepting and admitting a mistake costs you less than denying it. Honesty in error reduces consequences.

entries.luganda: Kaggwe ensonyi nga omukazi ayomba ne bba
entries.english: Shamelessness is when a woman quarrels with her husband in public.
entries.meaning: A woman who publicly quarrels with her husband shows a lack of humility and respect for the home. This proverb reflects traditional Baganda social expectations.
symbols: kaggwe ensonyi (one who is not shy)
entries.cultural_note: This reflects traditional gender dynamics in Buganda culture. Interpret with cultural context.

entries.english: The head of the house must be respected.
entries.meaning: The leader of a home or a workplace deserves respect and should be honored by those under them.

entries.english: A visitor should not act like they own the house.
entries.meaning: Guests should respect the rules and customs of the home they visit. Do not overstep your boundaries as a visitor.

entries.luganda: Ofunda nomubi ngazaala ngalima nga ayanilriza naabagenyi
entries.english: It is better to be with an ugly one who bears children, farms and is hospitable.
entries.meaning: Character and contribution matter far more than beauty. A hardworking and hospitable partner is more valuable than an attractive but unproductive one.
symbols: ofunda nomubi (rather be with ugly person)

entries.english: The banana plant fades the way you look after it.
entries.meaning: How you treat what you own determines how it thrives. A person — especially in marriage — will fade if not cared for and respected.

entries.luganda: Akaggo akaakuba muka balo bwokalaba okasuka mu muliro
entries.english: A stick that was used to beat your co-wife — when you see it, throw it in the fire.
entries.meaning: Get rid of things that caused harm or trouble in the past. Learn from others' painful experiences and do not repeat the same mistakes.

entries.english: What burns the poor also burns the rich.
entries.meaning: No one is exempt from consequences or suffering. Wealth and status do not protect you from the same challenges that affect everyone.

entries.english: Secrets of the house should stay inside the house.
entries.meaning: Family matters should be handled privately. Do not expose household problems to outsiders. What happens at home stays at home.

entries.english: The usefulness of a calabash is predicted by its handle.
entries.meaning: You can predict a person's future usefulness by their early behaviour. First impressions and early character reveal much about the future.

entries.english: The disease that catches the husband does not spare the wife.
entries.meaning: Partners share consequences. What affects one in a marriage affects the other. Be faithful and honest because your actions impact your spouse.

entries.english: An ungrateful person has no one to give them anything.
entries.meaning: Gratitude is essential. People who do not appreciate what they receive eventually find no one willing to help them.

entries.english: One you trained can chase you away.
entries.meaning: Someone you taught and raised up can eventually surpass and reject you. Do not underestimate those you mentor.

entries.luganda: Ekiddiba kidda wabwe wakyo, essaaniiko lidda much lusukku
entries.english: Leftovers get back to their owners; banana leaves return to the plantation.
entries.meaning: Everything finds its way back to where it belongs. People who leave always come home. Justice also returns to its source.

entries.english: An elder's eye sees even in a dirty place.
entries.meaning: Elders have experience and wisdom that allows them to see things others miss. They should be respected and their guidance heeded.

entries.luganda: Munno mu kabi, ye munno ddala
entries.english: Your friend in trouble is your true friend.
entries.meaning: Genuine friendship is revealed in hardship. Those who stand by you when things are difficult are your real allies.

entries.english: Go round the long way and arrive in safety.
entries.meaning: It is wiser to take a longer, safer path than to rush through a dangerous shortcut. Safety is worth the extra effort.

entries.english: People who live together cannot fail to have quarrels.
entries.meaning: Conflict is natural in any close relationship. Do not be surprised when people who share space disagree — it is normal.

entries.english: Relations are like beer-calabashes; they bump against each other but do not break.
entries.meaning: Family members will always have disagreements and conflicts, but the bonds of family are strong enough to survive them.

entries.english: Brandishing weapons does not affect the legs, but the mouth.
entries.meaning: The harm from boasting and threatening words falls back on the speaker. Boastful talk causes more harm to the one speaking than to the target.

entries.luganda: Ebukojja banjagala nga nyoko akyali mulamu
entries.english: Your mother's relatives only love you whilst she is still alive.
entries.meaning: Some relationships exist only because of a connecting person. When that person is gone, the bonds may dissolve. Value relationships built on genuine love.

entries.english: He who is not in love himself says they are infatuated.
entries.meaning: People who have not experienced something tend to judge it harshly. Do not criticize what you have not lived.

entries.luganda: Okwagalana kyayi kya mpiso, bwe kitasoba tikitunga
entries.english: Mutual love is like thread in a needle — if it does not pass through and stick out, it does not sew.
entries.meaning: Love must flow in both directions to be effective. One-sided love cannot build or hold a relationship together.

entries.english: Do not count what you have not yet got; they took back the bananas you had stolen at night.
entries.meaning: Do not assume you own something before it is secured. Premature counting of gains can lead to disappointment.

entries.luganda: Abalungi mbwa ya nnamaaso, bw'etebba n'eyigga
entries.english: The beautiful are like a dog with big eyes — might turn out to be a thief or a good hunter.
entries.meaning: Beauty tells you nothing about a person's true character. An attractive person may be harmful or helpful — you cannot know from looks alone.
symbols: mbwa ya nnamaaso (dog with big eyes)

entries.english: He who takes you at night, you thank him in the morning.
entries.meaning: Sometimes the value of someone's help is only understood later. Be grateful even for those who helped you in dark or difficult times.
